title: provision() resurrects TEARDOWN/EXPIRED rows but the already-queued teardown task drops them unconditionally (no state CAS)
claim: A TenantSchema in TEARDOWN (TTL janitor flip, or refresh's 30-min delayed teardown) is resurrected to ACTIVE by provision()'s IntegrityError fall-through (and its TTL reset, per the 2026-06-10 incident-b fix), but the already-queued teardown_schema/teardown_view_schema_task fetch the row by id and DROP SCHEMA CASCADE unconditionally with no re-check that state is still TEARDOWN — so a materialization that races the janitor (or that runs during the refresh 30-min window or queue backlog after a worker outage) loads fresh data into a schema the stale teardown then drops, flipping runs STALE and the row EXPIRED. Surviving sibling of incident-b: TTL was reset on resurrect but teardown was not made state-aware. Same race for view schemas vs rebuild_workspace_view_schema.

votes.evidence: Independently re-traced the full chain. (1) Resurrection: apps/workspaces/services/schema_manager.py:68-71 — provision() only matches existing rows with state__in=[ACTIVE, MATERIALIZING], so a TEARDOWN row is invisible; models.py:33 `schema_name = models.CharField(max_length=255, unique=True)` makes the create() at schema_manager.py:81-85 raise IntegrityError against the TEARDOWN row; lines 86-93 re-fetch and fall through for any non-ACTIVE/MATERIALIZING state; lines 120-122 unconditionally set `ts.state = SchemaState.ACTIVE; ts.last_accessed_at = timezone.now()` — resurrecting the row without dequeuing/cancelling the already-deferred teardown task. (2) Queued teardown with no CAS: apps/workspaces/tasks.py:609-620 — teardown_schema does `await TenantSchema.objects.aget(id=schema_id)` then `await asyncio.to_thread(manager.teardown, schema)` with NO re-check that state is still TEARDOWN; teardown() (schema_manager.py:194-201) runs `DROP SCHEMA IF EXISTS {} CASCADE`. After the drop, tasks.py:639-645 flips COMPLETED/PARTIAL MaterializationRuns to STALE, :653 fails dependent view schemas, :656-657 marks the row EXPIRED — exactly the claimed consequence for freshly-loaded data. (3) Two live arming paths: expire_inactive_schemas (tasks.py:516-553, periodic cron */30) flips ACTIVE→TEARDOWN and defers teardown_schema/teardown_view_schema_task; refresh_tenant_schema (tasks.py:186-197) flips old ACTIVE schemas to TEARDOWN and defers teardown_schema with schedule_in=30 minutes — a guaranteed 30-minute window during which provision() for the base schema name resurrects the row. (4) View-schema sibling: teardown_view_schema_task (tasks.py:587-606) has the same no-guard pattern and races rebuild_workspace_view_schema → build_view_schema, which sets vs.state=ACTIVE (schema_manager.py:436-438); workspace_service.py:66 also defers it unconditionally. (5) Reachability: provision() is called at mcp_server/services/materializer.py:183 inside run_pipeline, invoked by both materialize_workspace and refresh_tenant_schema tasks; the refresh endpoint is live (POST .../refresh/ → apps/workspaces/api/urls.py:22, defer at api/views.py:362-365). All paths are wired today.
votes.notes: Finding is accurate, including the "surviving sibling of incident-b" framing: provision() lines 114-122 carry the comment explaining the TTL reset added for the resurrect path, but neither teardown_schema nor teardown_view_schema_task was made state-aware. Two severity nuances: (a) the refresh path's 30-minute schedule_in window makes this considerably more reachable than a pure janitor-tick race — any re-materialization of the same tenant within 30 minutes of a refresh completing arms the bug deterministically, and worker-outage queue backlog (a previously observed prod failure mode, 2026-06-09) widens the janitor window arbitrarily; (b) "data-loss" is defensible but the lost data is materialized cache re-derivable from the upstream source via re-materialization, plus incorrect catalog state (runs STALE, row EXPIRED, dependent view schemas FAILED) — not permanent loss of source-of-truth data. Fix shape: state CAS in the teardown tasks (e.g. refuse to drop unless a conditional UPDATE ... WHERE state='teardown' wins, or re-check schema.state == TEARDOWN after aget and skip otherwise), applied to both teardown_schema and teardown_view_schema_task.

title: Partial-failure or cancel during multi-tenant rematerialization leaves the workspace's own view schema cascade-dropped but ACTIVE
claim: Per-tenant loads DROP TABLE ... CASCADE the tenant's raw tables, cascade-dropping the CURRENT workspace's namespaced ws_* views, but build_view_schema only runs when all_succeeded, and the sibling-rebuild explicitly excludes the current workspace. When one tenant fails or the user cancels mid-loop, the WorkspaceViewSchema stays ACTIVE missing the reloaded tenant's views; the resume's view_schema_failed check only fires when vs.state != ACTIVE, so the agent is told 'partial, answer what you can' / sources 'completed' while every query through the still-ACTIVE view schema hits missing views — the contradictory-catalog condition behind the #190 panic loops, whose canned 're-run materialization' advice happens to be the correct repair. PRs #227-#230 fixed this lie for sibling workspaces and the teardown path but missed the same-workspace partial/cancel path.

votes.evidence:
I re-traced the full chain from the worker entry point and reproduced every link.

1. DROP CASCADE of tenant tables. mcp_server/services/materializer.py:1419: `cur.execute(psql.SQL("DROP TABLE IF EXISTS {}.raw_visits CASCADE").format(sid))`, guarded by `if start_cursor is None:` (1418). The module docstring (lines 33-37) confirms the DROP only runs on fresh loads ("no DROP TABLE" on resume). build_view_schema (schema_manager.py:367-375) creates the workspace views as `CREATE VIEW ws_xxx.prefix__table AS SELECT * FROM schema_name.table_name` — they depend directly on the tenant's raw tables, so a `DROP TABLE ... CASCADE` cascade-drops the current workspace's namespaced views. The table is then recreated via `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` (materializer.py:1420) but the dropped view is NOT recreated (only build_view_schema does that).

2. build_view_schema is gated on all_succeeded. apps/workspaces/tasks.py:311 `all_succeeded = all(r.get("success") for r in tenant_results)` and 322 `if workspace_tenant_count > 1 and all_succeeded:` then `SchemaManager().build_view_schema(workspace)`. On MaterializationCancelled (285-288) the result is appended with success:False and the loop `break`s; on any Exception (307-309) success:False is appended — either makes all_succeeded False, so the current workspace's view schema is NOT rebuilt.

3. No pre-loop state transition of the current workspace's WorkspaceViewSchema. materialize_workspace (tasks.py:224-251) fetches the workspace and memberships and goes straight into the DROP/CREATE loop — it never flips the current workspace's view-schema row to MATERIALIZING/PROVISIONING. So the row stays whatever it was: ACTIVE if previously built.

4. Sibling rebuild excludes the current workspace and is the only path that runs unconditionally. _rebuild_sibling_view_schemas (tasks.py:441-456) is called unconditionally at 346-349 (outside the all_succeeded guard), but _sibling_view_schema_workspaces (417-438) does `.exclude(id=exclude_workspace_id)` (434). So siblings and the teardown path (PRs #227-#230) are repaired on partial/cancel, but the same workspace is not. This is exactly the asymmetry the finding names.

5. Resume's view_schema_failed only fires when vs.state != ACTIVE. resume_thread_after_materialization (tasks.py:1075-1083): `if status in ("completed", "partial"):` ... `vs = await WorkspaceViewSchema.objects.filter(workspace=workspace).afirst(); if vs is None or vs.state != SchemaState.ACTIVE: view_schema_failed = True`. Since the row is still ACTIVE (never touched), view_schema_failed stays False, so the "system-side fix required / do NOT re-run" branch (1085-1095) is skipped.

6. Misleading message reaches the agent, and resume always fires. The resume is deferred in the `finally` of materialize_workspace (tasks.py:356-360 -> _defer_resume_for_job at 363-395), so it fires on every partial/cancel. _aggregate_materialization_state (tasks.py:991-1015): a 2-tenant ws where A=COMPLETED, B=PARTIAL yields status="partial" -> resume body 1109-1119 "Answer what you can from the available data" while ws_<id> has zero live views. A FAILED or CANCELLED status falls through to the `else` at 1120-1125 ("Materialization just completed (status=...). Please continue ... using the now-loaded data") — also a lie. Every SQL query through the still-ACTIVE view schema then hits missing relations.

Reachability is live: materialize_workspace is dispatched from the MCP agent tool (mcp_server/server.py:607) and a REST endpoint (apps/workspaces/api/materialization_views.py:185); cancellation is reachable via two live endpoints (materialization_views.py:104 and jobs_cancel.py:30-36, which flip the run to CANCELLED that the worker checkpoint at tasks.py:493 reads to raise MaterializationCancelled).
votes.notes:
CONFIRMED with one minor scope correction in the finding's favor: the breakage is broader than "missing the reloaded tenant's views." Because build_view_schema is skipped entirely, ALL views that depend on ANY tenant whose tables were re-dropped during this run are gone — including tenants that loaded successfully before a later tenant failed/was cancelled (e.g. in [A COMPLETED, B FAILED], A's views are also cascade-dropped and never recreated). So the ACTIVE ws_<id> schema can end up with zero usable views, not just the failed tenant's subset.

Status/impact unchanged: LATENT/correctness is right. It is not BROKEN-NOW (requires the specific partial/cancel-during-rematerialization trigger and a previously-ACTIVE multi-tenant view schema), but all three preconditions are normal operations, so it is a genuine latent correctness defect that yields a contradictory catalog plus actively wrong agent guidance ("answer what you can" / "use the now-loaded data" over a broken query layer). The finding's note that the canned #190 "re-run materialization" advice happens to be the correct repair is consistent with the code: re-running with all tenants succeeding would hit build_view_schema (322) and rebuild the views. File:line references in the finding all check out (tasks.py:251-349, 417-438, 1063-1095; materializer.py:1418-1419).

title: build_view_schema reactivates EXPIRED view-schema rows without resetting last_accessed_at (incident-b class, view-schema edition)
claim: The 2026-06-10 TTL fix reset last_accessed_at in provision() but not in build_view_schema, which get_or_creates the WorkspaceViewSchema row regardless of state and on success saves only update_fields=['state','last_error'] — a row resurrected from EXPIRED keeps its >24h-old last_accessed_at; teardown_view_schema_task also leaves EXPIRED rows with old timestamps and touch_workspace_schemas only touches ACTIVE/MATERIALIZING rows, so nothing refreshes the timestamp before/during rebuild. expire_inactive_schemas then flips the freshly rebuilt view schema to TEARDOWN on its next */30 tick (window narrowed by atouch on each MCP call). Bonus: the comment claiming build_view_schema saves state=FAILED before re-raising is false for early ValueErrors raised before the row is fetched — rows can park in PROVISIONING with no last_error.

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced the full chain and reproduced every link.

1. build_view_schema does NOT reset last_accessed_at. schema_manager.py:280-287 `get_or_create`s the row "regardless of state", then unconditionally sets `vs.state = SchemaState.PROVISIONING; vs.save(update_fields=["schema_name", "state"])`. The success path at :436-438 is exactly `vs.state = SchemaState.ACTIVE; vs.last_error = ""; vs.save(update_fields=["state", "last_error"])` — last_accessed_at is never in any update_fields and never assigned. A row resurrected from EXPIRED keeps its prior (possibly >24h) timestamp.

2. teardown_view_schema_task leaves the stale timestamp. tasks.py:605-606: `vs.state = SchemaState.EXPIRED; await vs.asave(update_fields=["state"])` — last_accessed_at unchanged, so the value that triggered expiry is preserved into EXPIRED.

3. touch_workspace_schemas only refreshes ACTIVE/MATERIALIZING. workspace_service.py:105-110 filters `state__in=[SchemaState.ACTIVE, SchemaState.MATERIALIZING]` before `vs.atouch()`. An EXPIRED or PROVISIONING view schema is NOT touched by chat activity (chat/views.py:151 calls this on every message), so the user returning to an idle workspace does not refresh the row before/during rebuild.

4. expire_inactive_schemas is a LIVE periodic task that flips the rebuilt row. tasks.py:516 `@app.periodic(cron="*/30 * * * *")` (app = procrastinate app, tasks.py:33). At :547-553 it selects `state=SchemaState.ACTIVE, last_accessed_at__lt=cutoff` (cutoff = now - 24h, SCHEMA_TTL_HOURS=24 in base.py:345) and sets TEARDOWN + defers teardown_view_schema_task. So a freshly-rebuilt ACTIVE row carrying a >24h timestamp is torn down on the next ≤30-min tick.

5. Consequence proven by existing tests: tests/test_view_schema_ttl.py:32-51 (`test_expire_inactive_schemas_also_expires_stale_view_schemas`) sets an ACTIVE view schema's last_accessed_at to now-25h and asserts expire_inactive_schemas flips it to TEARDOWN — exactly the state build_view_schema leaves a resurrected row in. :69-81 confirms null timestamps are never expired (so brand-new rows with last_accessed_at=None are safe; only previously-touched rows that reached EXPIRED are vulnerable — narrowing but not eliminating the bug).

6. Asymmetry confirmed. provision() at schema_manager.py:120-122 explicitly does `ts.state = ACTIVE; ts.last_accessed_at = timezone.now(); ts.save(update_fields=["state", "last_accessed_at"])`, with comment at :116-119 naming exactly this "expire_inactive_schemas drops it on its next tick" bug, and a dedicated regression test tests/test_schema_manager.py:84-122 (`test_provision_resurrects_expired_schema_and_refreshes_ttl`, asserts `ts.last_accessed_at > stale`). build_view_schema got NEITHER the fix NOR a test: an awk scan of all build_view_schema test bodies in test_schema_manager.py yields 0 references to last_accessed_at.

7. Bonus (false comment) confirmed. The early raises in build_view_schema — `if not tenants: raise ValueError` (:259-260) and the per-tenant "has no active schema" `raise ValueError` (:270-274) — occur BEFORE the get_or_create at :280 and before the try block at :289 whose except saves state=FAILED (:428-431). So those raises persist no FAILED state. The comment at tasks.py:573-575 ("build_view_schema already saves state=FAILED before re-raising") is false for them. And because add_workspace_tenant (workspace_service.py:25-27) and remove_workspace_tenant (:68-70) set the EXISTING row to PROVISIONING externally and THEN defer the rebuild, a row whose rebuild hits an early ValueError parks in PROVISIONING with no last_error — confirmed.
votes.notes: Status LATENT is correct, not BROKEN-NOW: triggering requires the specific (but realistic) sequence multi-tenant workspace idle >24h so the view schema reaches EXPIRED with a stale non-null timestamp, then a rebuild (sibling re-materialization via tasks.py:456, own materialize at :322, or add/remove tenant). The post-rebuild window is narrowed by two things the finding correctly names: (a) MCP load_workspace_context atouch at mcp_server/context.py:125 refreshes the timestamp on the next agent DB call, and (b) the */30 cadence of expire_inactive_schemas. If an MCP call lands before the next tick, the row survives; otherwise the just-rebuilt view schema is torn down and the agent gets "No active view schema for workspace" on its next query. Impact is correctness (a freshly-provisioned resource is silently destroyed); no data loss since underlying tenant tables persist and a subsequent rebuild can recreate the views. Finding's files/line ranges, mechanism, reviewers' framing, and replication=2 all check out.

title: No per-tenant mutual exclusion for materialization: thread-scoped guard permits concurrent DROP/CREATE/INSERT on a shared tenant schema
claim: The in-flight dispatch guard filters ThreadJob by thread_id only; an in-code comment admits the materializer has no advisory lock per tenant_schema, so two threads in one workspace, two workspaces sharing a tenant, or a /materialize/retry/ racing a chat dispatch can run parallel run_pipeline against the same physical tenant schema. Interleaved DROP/CREATE/INSERT and per-page commits from two workers can deadlock or produce mixed table contents with both runs recording COMPLETED; the no-natural-PK resumable Connect tables can duplicate. The dead MATERIALIZING state (separate finding) removes the only single-tenant in-progress signal that might have deterred a second dispatch.

votes.evidence:
GUARD IS THREAD-SCOPED (confirmed): mcp_server/server.py:586-590 — `ThreadJob.objects.filter(thread_id=thread_id, job_type=...MATERIALIZATION, state__in=list(ThreadJob.ACTIVE_STATES))`. The in-code comment at server.py:578-585 admits it verbatim: "this lets two threads in the same workspace dispatch parallel materializations that share tenant_schemas... and the materializer has no advisory lock per tenant_schema."

RETRY PATH (confirmed): apps/workspaces/api/materialization_views.py:171-182 dedupes only against the supplied thread's ThreadJobs; with no thread_id in the body it skips dedupe entirely and dispatches unconditionally (lines 153, 184-195: "if not thread_id: return JsonResponse({'status': 'started', ...})" after defer_async).

SHARED PHYSICAL SCHEMA (confirmed): apps/workspaces/services/schema_manager.py:57-78 — provision() resolves schema by deterministic `_sanitize_schema_name(tenant.external_id)` (line 66) and returns the existing ACTIVE/MATERIALIZING record (lines 68-78); two concurrent run_pipeline calls for one tenant write to the SAME schema. materializer.py:183 calls `SchemaManager().provision(tenant_membership.tenant)`.

NO LOCK ANYWHERE (confirmed): apps/workspaces/tasks.py:203 — `@task(pass_context=True)` with no queueing_lock; grep for advisory/pg_advisory/queueing_lock across mcp_server and apps returns nothing.

DESTRUCTIVE INTERLEAVING SURFACE (confirmed): materializer.py:1419 `DROP TABLE IF EXISTS {}.raw_visits CASCADE` then CREATE/INSERT per source; resumable writers "commit per page" (materializer.py:1404-1405). raw_completed_works/raw_payments/raw_invoices use `id BIGINT GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY` (no natural key) — two runs resuming from the same FAILED prior cursor (_load_prior_resume_cursors, materializer.py:252, 567-570) would insert identical pages as duplicate rows with both runs succeeding. Note raw_visits DOES have a natural PK (`visit_id BIGINT PRIMARY KEY`, materializer.py:1424), so the duplication sub-claim applies to the identity-PK tables, not visits.

REACHABILITY CORRECTION (refutes "race is live today"): all run_pipeline executions are procrastinate jobs (server.py:607 and materialization_views.py:185 both go through materialize_workspace.defer_async; refresh path tasks.py:173 also runs in-worker). Production runs ONE worker host with `cmd: "python manage.py procrastinate worker"` and no --concurrency flag (config/deploy-worker.yml), and procrastinate 3.8.1's Worker.__init__ concurrency default is 1 (verified via inspect.signature). Duplicate dispatches therefore SERIALIZE at the worker today; a serial second run is wasteful but safe (clean run does DROP+CREATE; COMPLETED runs excluded from resume cursors, materializer.py:249-252). Concurrent interleaving requires worker scale-out, a --concurrency/PROCRASTINATE_WORKER_CONCURRENCY change, or transient old/new worker container overlap during a kamal deploy.
votes.notes: The defect is real and the mechanism is accurately described — there is no per-tenant mutual exclusion anywhere in code, and the code comment itself concedes it. But the finding overstates current exposure: with the production topology (single worker, procrastinate default concurrency=1), two run_pipeline invocations cannot execute concurrently, so the DROP/CREATE/INSERT interleave and concurrent-duplicate scenarios are not reachable today. The protection is an accident of deployment config with zero code-level enforcement — one `--concurrency 2` flag, a second worker replica, or a deploy-window container overlap makes it live, hence LATENT rather than DEBT (it is a correctness hazard waiting on a config change, not merely maintainability debt). Two further precision corrections: (1) raw_visits — the largest Connect table — has a natural PK (visit_id), so the duplicate-rows risk is limited to the identity-PK resumable tables (completed_works, payments, invoices, and similar); (2) even today, the thread-scoped guard does permit duplicate *serial* runs (wasted full re-pull of upstream data per duplicate dispatch), which is a real cost-perf annoyance but not data loss. Recommended fix matches the comment's own suggestion: tenant-scoped dedupe at dispatch plus a pg_advisory_xact_lock keyed on tenant_schema inside run_pipeline as defense in depth.

title: get_schema_status reads an extinct result shape: single-tenant 'tables' always [] after a successful run
claim: server.py reads last_run.result['tables'] or legacy result['table']+'rows_loaded', but run_pipeline/the materializer persist only {pipeline, sources, transforms} and top-level rows_loaded — neither branch matches, so single-tenant get_schema_status always returns exists=True/state=active/tables=[] even after a fully successful materialization, disagreeing with list_tables in the same conversation. Rename residue from the pre-pipeline result shape; mitigated because the prompt steers the agent to list_tables. (Distinct from the broader catalog-divergence cluster: this is the specific dead-key bug.)

votes.notes: Status DEBT and impact correctness are both correct. It is a dead-key contract-drift bug yielding a wrong-but-non-crashing answer (tables=[] with exists=True/state=active) on a tool the agent is steered away from but can still call. Not BROKEN-NOW (prompt pre-fetch + list_tables steering keep it from biting on the common path) and not LATENT (the branch is live code, not gated). The mitigation claim ("prompt steers to list_tables") is verified in base_system.py and base.py:757. Distinct from the broader catalog-divergence cluster as claimed — this specific bug is the never-matching key read in the single-tenant branch, whereas the cluster findings (phantom tables, no live reconciliation in get_schema_status) are about reading run.result without information_schema reconciliation at all. Both are true; this finding is the narrower dead-key subset.

title: SchemaState.MATERIALIZING is never written by production code: ~15 readers branch on a dead state; single-tenant in-progress guard unreachable
claim: No code anywhere in apps/ or mcp_server/ assigns MATERIALIZING (grep-verified; only test fixtures set it; provision() goes PROVISIONING->ACTIVE before any data loads), yet 8-15 readers filter/branch on it (context routing, prompt assembly, status derivation, availability checks, get_schema_status's ACTIVE|MATERIALIZING filter that mismatches load_workspace_context's ACTIVE-only aget). The single-tenant prompt branch 'materialization already in progress — do NOT trigger another one' can never fire, so during an in-flight single-tenant load the prompt says data is loaded but no tables yet and list_tables appends 'Run run_materialization', steering a second thread into a parallel dispatch (the only deterrent the concurrency finding lacked). The multi-tenant twin correctly checks MaterializationRun.ACTIVE_STATES; 'available' status is reported during DROP/recreate windows.

votes.evidence:
Core factual claim CONFIRMED by independent re-trace. grep for assignments (state = SchemaState.MATERIALIZING) across apps/ and mcp_server/ returned ZERO production hits; only 3 test files set it (tests/test_workspace_service.py:133, tests/agents/test_schema_context.py:46,300). The state is defined at apps/workspaces/models.py:18 and read at ~15 sites (verified: base.py:213,230,325; schema_manager.py:70,90; mcp_server/server.py:694,740; mcp_server/context.py:58; workspace_views.py:85,249; api/views.py:36,55; workspace_service.py:92,102,107; recipes/api/views.py:119; backfill cmd:32).

Unreachable single-tenant guard CONFIRMED. apps/workspaces/tasks.py:125-184 refresh_tenant_schema creates a PROVISIONING TenantSchema, runs the pipeline while STILL PROVISIONING (line 173 run_pipeline), then flips directly to ACTIVE at line 182 after the load completes — MATERIALIZING is never assigned. Therefore apps/agents/graph/base.py:230 `if ts.state == SchemaState.MATERIALIZING:` can never fire from production data. This function IS live: called at base.py:758 inside _build_system_prompt (base.py:699), invoked at runtime per base.py:525. Multi-tenant twin (base.py:320-325) correctly uses MaterializationRun.ACTIVE_STATES (models.py:73-80) so the asymmetry the finding describes is real.

HOWEVER, the severity/mechanism is overstated on two points:

1. The 'second thread steered into parallel dispatch (the only deterrent the concurrency finding lacked)' claim is undercut by mcp_server/server.py:586-604: run_materialization has a ThreadJob ACTIVE_STATES guard that returns status=already_in_progress. The surrounding comment (server.py:572-585) explicitly documents the guard is thread-scoped and that cross-thread parallel dispatch is a PRE-EXISTING, known condition unrelated to this dead state. So same-thread re-trigger is already blocked by ThreadJob; the dead MATERIALIZING guard would, at most, have produced a friendlier prompt — it is not the load-bearing deterrent the finding implies.

2. The get_schema_status vs load_workspace_context 'mismatch' is mostly wrong/benign. SINGLE-tenant: get_schema_status (server.py:694) uses ACTIVE|MATERIALIZING and load_workspace_context delegates to load_tenant_context (context.py:58) which ALSO uses ACTIVE|MATERIALIZING — they MATCH. The only real divergence is MULTI-tenant (server.py:740 ACTIVE|MATERIALIZING vs context.py:117 ACTIVE-only aget), and because MATERIALIZING is never written, it produces ZERO observable behavioral difference today. Latent debt, not a live correctness fault.
votes.notes: Recommend keeping status=DEBT, impact=correctness. The structural defect (dead enum state + asymmetric/unreachable single-tenant guard) is genuine and worth fixing (either wire MATERIALIZING into the refresh path, or replace base.py:230's check with a MaterializationRun.ACTIVE_STATES query mirroring the multi-tenant twin). But two of the finding's sharper claims do not hold: (a) the cited 'parallel dispatch' risk is already mitigated within a thread by the ThreadJob guard at server.py:586-604, and the cross-thread gap is documented as a separate pre-existing issue; (b) the get_schema_status/load_workspace_context 'mismatch' is matched in the single-tenant path and behaviorally inert everywhere because the state is never set. The 'available during DROP/recreate' aside concerns WorkspaceViewSchema rebuild (schema_manager.py:358-365), a different mechanism than the MATERIALIZING dead-state. Net: real DEBT, misstated mechanism/severity → PARTIAL.

title: Retry/error-shape hardening applied only to Connect loaders; OCS/CommCare swallow malformed responses and have no retry
claim: connect_base has bounded urllib3 Retry (backoff, Retry-After, status forcelist) and raises ConnectExportError on missing 'results'/invalid JSON; ocs_base and commcare_base call raise_for_status with no retry, so one transient 5xx fails an entire (non-resumable) source and rolls back everything loaded — OCS messages' N+1 per-session GETs compound exposure and a single deleted-session 404 aborts the whole source. ocs_base._paginate and commcare_cases treat a missing 'results'/'cases' key as an empty page, yielding a source marked completed with 0 rows (silent-empty on the exact envelope-change class that bit Connect before validation was added); _normalize_case defaults every field to ''. CommCare cases also missed the relative-next-URL fix (8774864) applied to forms/metadata, so a non-absolute next URL raises MissingSchema and fails cases.

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced every sub-claim against the actual files; line numbers in the finding are essentially accurate.

RETRY ASYMMETRY (CONFIRMED): connect_base.py:61-69 `_build_retry()` builds urllib3 Retry(total=3, backoff_factor=2.0, status_forcelist=[500,502,503,504,408,429], respect_retry_after_header=True, raise_on_status=False), mounted on the session at lines 128-130. ocs_base.py has NO HTTPAdapter/Retry — plain `requests.Session()` (line 39) and `resp.raise_for_status()` at lines 51 and 73. commcare_base.py has NO retry — plain session (line 46), `resp.raise_for_status()` at line 68. `grep -rn 'HTTPAdapter|Retry|mount(' mcp_server/loaders/` returns hits ONLY in connect_base. So OCS/CommCare get zero retries on transient 5xx.

ERROR-SHAPE HARDENING (CONFIRMED): connect_base.py:217-223 raises ConnectExportError on invalid JSON (`except ValueError`) and on `if "results" not in payload`. ocs_base.py:75 does `payload.get("results", [])` and commcare_cases.py:52 does `data.get("cases", [])` — a missing key silently yields an empty page; if `next` is also absent the loop terminates and the writer returns 0 rows. materializer.py:376-378 then marks that source `state="completed", rows=0` with no zero-row guard. This is exactly the silent-empty envelope-change class Connect now rejects.

ROLLBACK/REACHABILITY (CONFIRMED, scope clarified): loaders are wired into the live materializer (imports at materializer.py:57-72; dispatch in _load_source/_load_ocs_source/_load_connect_source). For non-resumable sources (ALL OCS, ALL CommCare cases/forms, and Connect `users`) _load_and_commit_source (676-729) runs the whole source in one transaction, commits only at line 713-714, and on ANY exception calls conn.rollback() at 718 — so a single transient failure discards everything loaded *for that source*. The run loop (328-371) keeps earlier-committed sources and marks the run PARTIAL/FAILED; it does NOT roll back other sources. The finding's phrase "rolls back everything loaded" is correct read per-source, but a reader could mistake it for cross-source — worth the clarification.

OCS MESSAGES N+1 / 404 (CONFIRMED): ocs_messages.py:46-48 loops session_ids and calls self._get(detail_url); OCSBaseLoader._get (ocs_base.py:45-52) calls resp.raise_for_status(), so a single deleted-session 404 raises HTTPError that propagates and aborts the entire messages source (which already wrote nothing durable, since messages is non-resumable). Confirmed.

_normalize_case (CONFIRMED): commcare_cases.py:77-90 defaults case_id/case_type/external_id/owner_id and all date fields to "".

RELATIVE-NEXT-URL ASYMMETRY (CONFIRMED as code asymmetry, OVERSTATED as active bug): `git show 8774864` confirms the commit "fix(commcare): correctly resolve relative meta.next URLs" added _resolve_next_url to commcare_base and applied it in forms.py:70 and metadata.py:55. commcare_cases.py:68 still does the raw `url = data.get("next")` and does NOT call _resolve_next_url. So a relative `next` from the case endpoint WOULD reach `self._session.get(<relative>)` and raise requests.MissingSchema. BUT: the 8774864 fix targeted the tastypie `meta.next` family (`/api/v0.5/form/`, `/api/v0.5/application/`), whereas cases hits the newer Case API v2 (`/api/case/v2/`) which returns a top-level `next` cursor URL. Every test fixture (tests/test_commcare_loader.py:33,80) and the mcp_server test (test_mcp_server.py:261) models Case API v2's `next` as ABSOLUTE. There is no evidence in the repo that Case API v2 ever emits a relative cursor URL. So the MissingSchema consequence is a speculative LATENT inconsistency, not a demonstrated active defect like the others.
votes.notes: Recommend PARTIAL. The retry/error-shape/silent-empty/rollback/N+1-404/normalize-default sub-claims are all independently CONFIRMED with correct mechanism and live reachability (materializer pipeline). The single over-reach is the cases relative-next-URL → MissingSchema sub-claim: the fix it references (8774864) was for tastypie meta.next, while cases uses Case API v2 whose next is a top-level cursor URL modeled as absolute everywhere in the test suite. That sub-claim is a defensive inconsistency worth fixing for robustness but is LATENT/unproven, not the same demonstrated-DEBT class as the rest. Overall status DEBT / impact correctness is appropriate for the bundle; the title is accurate. No severity inflation beyond the one speculative sub-claim. Files re-verified: mcp_server/loaders/connect_base.py:61-69,128-130,217-223; ocs_base.py:45-52,54-85; ocs_messages.py:34-58; commcare_base.py:49-69; commcare_cases.py:52,68,77-90; commcare_forms.py:70; commcare_metadata.py:55; materializer.py:676-729,328-381.

title: Resumability truth lives in two contradictory registries (_RESUMABLE_CONNECT_SOURCES vs YAML) with an unsafe default-True; non-resumable Connect writers still commit per page
claim: Resumability is decided in run_pipeline from source.resumable AND the hardcoded set _RESUMABLE_CONNECT_SOURCES, which still lists the five sources (completed_works, payments, invoices, assessments, completed_modules) that commit 2587158 made non-resumable in connect_sync.yml (resumable:false). YAML currently wins via the AND, but pipeline_registry defaults resumable=True, so a new Connect source without an explicit YAML flag gets cursor-resume against a plain-INSERT (no ON CONFLICT) writer, silently reintroducing the f26c1a0 row-duplication class. Independently, all 7 Connect writers commit DROP/CREATE up-front and commit per page regardless of the flag, violating _load_and_commit_source's documented single-transaction contract for non-resumable sources: a mid-load failure/cancel durably leaves a partial/empty table while the run records rows:0 — readable via the query tool (catalog hides only state!=completed). The constant's docstring is false and the dead resume plumbing/'Resumable:' docstrings invite re-enablement; replay tests use id-less fixtures so nothing would catch it.

votes.evidence:
I re-traced the full chain independently against the live code.

TWO SEPARATE REGISTRIES (confirmed, but finding misstates how they combine):
- `run_pipeline` decides resumability ONLY from `source_is_resumable = is_resumable_provider and source.resumable` (materializer.py:264). It NEVER references `_RESUMABLE_CONNECT_SOURCES`. grep confirms the frozenset has exactly two references: its definition (772) and one use at 803.
- `_RESUMABLE_CONNECT_SOURCES = frozenset({"visits","completed_works","payments","invoices","assessments","completed_modules"})` (materializer.py:772-774) is consulted independently inside `_load_connect_source` (line 803) to pick the WRITER CALL PATH, not in run_pipeline. So the finding's central mechanism sentence ("Resumability is decided in run_pipeline from source.resumable AND the hardcoded set") is wrong — they are two independent decisions in two functions, not an AND in run_pipeline.

YAML vs frozenset disagreement (confirmed): connect_sync.yml:17-31 marks completed_works/payments/invoices/assessments/completed_modules `resumable:false`, yet all 5 remain in the frozenset. pipeline_registry.py:25,124 default resumable=True. git show 2587158 confirms it dropped ON CONFLICT(id) and marked these 5 non-resumable; f26c1a0 confirms it had added id PRIMARY KEY + ON CONFLICT(id) DO NOTHING to fix duplication.

PER-PAGE COMMITS ON NON-RESUMABLE (confirmed): For the 5 sources, run_pipeline passes resumable=False, start_cursor=None, cursor_callback=None. But because they ARE in the frozenset, line 803 routes them through the per-page-commit writer path (804-812). Each writer commits DROP/CREATE up front (e.g. 1621, 1710, 1790, 1862, 1932) and commits per page (e.g. 1656, 1741, 1815, 1887, 1955). The 5 INSERTs are plain INSERT with NO ON CONFLICT (1290-1335). _load_and_commit_source docstring (689-697) documents a single-transaction contract for resumable=False that these writers violate; line 714's outer commit is a redundant no-op since the writer already committed per-page.

PARTIAL-TABLE-READABLE CONSEQUENCE (confirmed reachable): On mid-load failure run_pipeline sets source state "failed", rows preserved (337-339; test_materializer.py:556 asserts rows==0). pipeline_list_tables hides only state!="completed" (metadata.py:80) so the partial physical table is excluded from the catalog. But the `query` MCP tool (server.py:333-375) executes arbitrary SELECT against ctx.schema_name and NEVER consults the catalog — so `SELECT count(*) FROM raw_completed_works` reads the partial/empty table directly. Chain is wired live: run_materialization tool (server.py:521) -> materialize_workspace procrastinate task -> run_pipeline (tasks.py:173,496) for commcare_connect tenants.

TEST GAP (confirmed): test_materializer.py:1199-1343 feed id-less fixtures and test only happy-path full reload (start_cursor=None, DROP/CREATE) idempotency; they do not exercise mid-load failure durability or any resume duplication.

DUPLICATION-REINTRODUCTION CLAIM (REFUTED as stated): The finding's most alarming claim — "a new Connect source without an explicit YAML flag gets cursor-resume against a plain-INSERT writer, silently reintroducing duplication" — does not hold. A new source defaulting to resumable=True still does NOT engage cursor-resume, because _load_connect_source forwards start_cursor/cursor_callback ONLY when the source is in _RESUMABLE_CONNECT_SOURCES (803-812); the else branch (814) drops them. It would also raise ValueError (797-799) unless added to loader_map with a writer. So default-True alone cannot reintroduce the f26c1a0 class — that requires ALSO adding the source to the frozenset and giving it a no-ON-CONFLICT writer. For the current 5 sources, start_cursor is always None so every run DROP/CREATEs (line 1593 guard), and within a run pages are distinct — no duplication today.
votes.notes: Keep status DEBT and impact correctness. The genuine, reachable defects: (1) two contradictory resumability registries (frozenset vs YAML) that must be manually kept in sync; (2) non-resumable Connect writers commit DROP/CREATE up front and per page, so a mid-load failure/cancel durably leaves a partial/empty table while the run records rows:0 — and that table is readable via the live `query` tool even though pipeline_list_tables hides it; (3) the _load_and_commit_source single-transaction docstring is false for these 5 sources; (4) tests only cover happy-path full reload, not partial-failure durability. TWO finding claims are wrong and should be corrected before acting on it: (a) run_pipeline does NOT decide resumability via an AND with the frozenset — it uses only source.resumable; the frozenset is a separate writer-path gate in _load_connect_source. (b) The headlined default-True row-duplication reintroduction is NOT reachable from a YAML flag alone; the frozenset independently gates the cursor plumbing, so a new default-True source gets the non-resumable writer path with start_cursor/cursor_callback dropped. The real risk is the latent inconsistency/footgun and the partial-table durability leak, not an active duplication path — so this is genuine DEBT, not a BROKEN-NOW correctness bug. The dead resume plumbing and the misleading 'Resumable:' writer docstrings for the 5 frozenset members are a legitimate re-enablement footgun worth cleaning up (remove the 5 from the frozenset and/or delete the dead start_cursor branches), but no current data-loss/duplication is occurring.

title: Minor run-lifecycle drift: terminal-run progress writes, unconditional result clobber, post-COMPLETED step-guard raise; materialized_row_count wrong after resumed run
claim: progress_updater writes progress unconditionally onto terminal runs; the cancel-before-transform branch unconditionally overwrites result; the step-count guard raises AFTER the run is marked COMPLETED and the schema ACTIVE (a drift would report tenant failure while the row says COMPLETED); cancel_materialization's docstring documents the FAILED drift as intended. On resume the visits writer restarts total=0 so the completion entry records only this run's increment (catalog reports e.g. 10k for a 60k-row table via materialized_row_count, repeated in status summaries; mitigated only by row_count_verified:false), and visits resume re-mixes mutable status/review_status fields the ON CONFLICT exists to refresh without re-fetching behind the watermark.

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced every sub-claim from live entry points. The chain is reachable: MCP tool `run_materialization` (mcp_server/server.py:521) defers `materialize_workspace` (apps/workspaces/tasks.py:204), which calls `_run_pipeline_with_progress` (tasks.py:466) -> `run_pipeline` (materializer.py). The canceller `cancel_materialization` is a registered MCP tool (server.py:445-446).

(1) Terminal-run progress writes — CONFIRMED. tasks.py:489 `MaterializationRun.objects.filter(id=run_id).update(progress=progress)` is filtered by id only, no state guard, so a late page callback writes `progress` even onto a terminal run. It only touches the `progress` JSONField (models.py:91), not state/result — hence cosmetic.

(2) Unconditional result clobber in cancel-before-transform branch — CONFIRMED. materializer.py:435-443: the CAS `filter(id, state=LOADING).update(state=TRANSFORMING)` and on miss (`if not rows`) line 439-441 does `filter(id=run.id).update(result={"cancelled": True, "sources": source_results})` — id-only filter, no state guard, unconditionally overwriting `result`.

(3) Step-count guard raises AFTER COMPLETED + ACTIVE — CONFIRMED. The COMPLETE block at materializer.py:471-477 CAS-updates state=COMPLETED, then 485-486 sets `tenant_schema.state = "active"` and saves, then 488 computes total_rows, then 491-495 `if step != total_steps: raise RuntimeError(...)`. So the assertion raises while the DB row is COMPLETED and the schema is ACTIVE — a drift would report tenant failure though the row says COMPLETED. (This is a developer assertion against miscounted report() calls; total_steps = 2 + len(sources) + 1 at line 125.)

(4) cancel docstring documents FAILED drift — CONFIRMED, and stronger than stated. cancel_materialization (server.py:447-451 docstring: "Marks the run as failed", line 479 `run.state = FAILED`) sets FAILED, NOT CANCELLED. Meanwhile materializer comments (lines 238, 388, 463, 611) and the progress_updater check (tasks.py:493 `if current_state == CANCELLED`) all reference CANCELLED. Consequence: the live cancel path never produces CANCELLED — MaterializationCancelled only ever raises from CAS-miss branches AFTER the canceller already wrote FAILED. This is exactly the "FAILED drift documented as intended" the finding describes.

(5) Resume undercount via materialized_row_count — CONFIRMED. On resume, `_write_connect_visits` (materializer.py:1453) starts `total=0` and only increments newly-fetched pages (1488), returning this-run-only total. run_pipeline records `source_results[name] = {"state":"completed","rows": rows,...}` (376-381) with that partial total. metadata.py:90 sets `materialized_row_count = source_data.get("rows")` (the partial) with `row_count_verified: False` (line 91), and tasks.py:971 `materialized_row_counts[source] = info["rows"]` repeats it in status summaries. So a 60k-row table loaded as 50k+10k records 10k. Mitigated by row_count_verified:false + base_system.py prompt rules forbidding the agent from surfacing the number.

(6) Visits resume re-mixes mutable fields behind watermark — CONFIRMED. visits is in _RESUMABLE_CONNECT_SOURCES (770-774); the loader uses forward-only keyset pagination via last_id (connect_visits.py:72-77 -> connect_base.py:180-182 `request_params["last_id"]=start_last_id`), fetching only id>start_cursor. The ON CONFLICT (visit_id) DO UPDATE SET status/form_json/review_status/images (materializer.py:1271-1273) exists to refresh those mutable fields, but on a forward-only resume rows behind the watermark are never re-fetched, so their mutable status/review_status stay stale.
votes.notes: Every sub-claim reproduced independently at the cited line ranges (line numbers all accurate). Severity COSMETIC / impact correctness is appropriate and not understated: each user-visible consequence is mitigated. (a) The undercount is gated behind row_count_verified:false plus explicit base_system.py prompt rules instructing the agent to never surface materialized_row_count as an answer. (b) The step-count guard is a developer assertion that only trips if a code change adds/removes a report() call without updating total_steps — not a data/runtime condition. (c) The terminal-run progress write and result clobber only touch advisory JSON fields, not the authoritative terminal state. One nuance that strengthens rather than weakens the finding: the live canceller sets FAILED (server.py:479), so the codebase's many CANCELLED comments/checks (tasks.py:493, materializer.py:238/388/463/611) are aspirational drift — MaterializationCancelled is effectively unreachable via the real cancel endpoint and only raises from CAS-miss branches after FAILED is already written. The finding captured this as the documented 'FAILED drift'. No refutation found; reachability is real (live MCP tools + procrastinate task). The stale-mutable-fields issue (6) is the most substantive correctness concern but is inherent to the #187 incremental-resume design and bounded to visits' status/review_status/form_json/images behind the watermark.

title: No janitor reconciles MaterializationRun rows stuck in ACTIVE states after hard worker death; procrastinate stalled-job rescue never wired
claim: A worker that dies hard (SIGKILL after deploy stop-timeout, OOM, host crash) leaves the procrastinate job row 'doing' forever and the MaterializationRun in DISCOVERING/LOADING/TRANSFORMING forever: reconcile_stale_thread_job returns None for status in {todo,doing} (correct per-tick, permanent for zombies) and no code calls procrastinate 3.8's get_stalled_jobs/heartbeats/retry_job (grep: zero 'stalled' hits) and prune_stalled_workers never requeues jobs. No janitor iterates MaterializationRun rows at all. UI shows a frozen progress banner/spinner indefinitely (only escape is manual Stop then Retry); zombie rows block resume cursors from prior PARTIAL runs and are miscounted as active by the cancel endpoint. deploy-worker.yml has no stop_wait/shutdown-graceful-timeout and materializations routinely exceed docker's grace period, so every worker deploy mid-materialization re-arms this — the still-open 2026-05-30 zombie-doing incident class; June-10 fixed the dead-CONNECTION trigger, not the dead-PROCESS one. The docker-compose stack ships no worker at all (CLAUDE.md advertises 'all services'), so every materialization on the self-host compose path hangs forever.

votes.evidence:
Re-traced the full chain from worker death to frozen UI/blocked retry, reading every file myself.

1. Zombie 'doing' is permanent in procrastinate unless app code rescues it. .venv/.../procrastinate/sql/schema.sql:77 `worker_id bigint REFERENCES procrastinate_workers(id) ON DELETE SET NULL` — pruning a stalled worker only NULLs the job's worker_id, never resets status. .venv/.../procrastinate/worker.py:379-384: `Worker.run()` calls `prune_stalled_workers(...)` (delete worker rows) and nothing else; no call to `get_stalled_jobs`/`retry_job` anywhere in the worker loop. Those rescue APIs exist (manager.py:223 `get_stalled_jobs`, :462 `retry_job`) but are opt-in. Installed version: procrastinate 3.8.1 (uv.lock:2015).

2. Scout never wires the rescue. `grep -rn "stalled" apps/ mcp_server/ config/` over real code returns only false positives on the substring "installed" (test_auth.py etc.) — zero calls to get_stalled_jobs/retry_job/heartbeat APIs anywhere in the repo.

3. Both janitors punt on todo/doing forever. apps/workspaces/tasks.py:748-749: `if status in {"todo", "doing"}: return None` inside `reconcile_stale_thread_job`. Callers: worker-side periodic `expire_stale_thread_jobs` (tasks.py:819-838, cron */15) and the API-side poll backstop `apps/workspaces/api/jobs_views.py:123-135`. Both call the same function, so a job row frozen at 'doing' (dead process) or 'todo' (no worker at all) leaves the ThreadJob in ACTIVE_STATES indefinitely and the frontend (`useWorkspaceJobs` polls this endpoint, jobs_views.py:90) keeps rendering it as active. The June-10 fix (API-side backstop) only covers the dead-CONNECTION case where the job row transitions to failed; it cannot help when the row itself is stuck at 'doing' — finding's "dead-PROCESS vs dead-CONNECTION" distinction is accurate.

4. No janitor touches MaterializationRun active rows. tasks.py:516-553 (`cleanup_expired_schemas`, cron */30) docstring at :524 explicitly: "The data-bearing MaterializationRun rows are NOT touched here." The only other state flips are teardown's COMPLETED/PARTIAL→STALE (tasks.py:639-645). MaterializationRun.ACTIVE_STATES = {started, discovering, loading, transforming} (apps/workspaces/models.py:73-80); nothing ever reconciles rows stuck in those states.

5. Downstream consequences of zombie ACTIVE rows — confirmed and actually worse than claimed:
   - apps/agents/graph/base.py:318-332: any MaterializationRun in ACTIVE_STATES makes the agent context say "A materialization is already in progress... Do NOT trigger another one" — the agent permanently refuses new materializations while the zombie exists (finding didn't cite this; it strengthens the case).
   - Cancel endpoint counts zombies as active: apps/workspaces/api/materialization_views.py:46-54 filters ACTIVE_STATES; apps/workspaces/api/jobs_cancel.py:30-36 flips them to CANCELLED — so manual Stop is indeed the only escape, as claimed.
   - Resume cursors: mcp_server/services/materializer.py:583-593 `_load_prior_resume_cursors` takes only the single most-recent prior run and returns {} unless THAT run is PARTIAL/FAILED. A zombie run stuck in an active state (or CANCELLED after manual Stop) shadows an older PARTIAL run, discarding its cursors — confirmed mechanism (re-load-from-scratch cost, not data loss).

6. Deploy re-arms it: config/deploy-worker.yml has no stop/grace configuration of any kind (whole file read; cmd at line 16 is `python manage.py procrastinate worker` with no `--shutdown-graceful-timeout`). A deploy that exceeds docker's stop grace SIGKILLs the worker mid-job → 'doing' zombie.

7. Compose path: docker-compose.yml defines exactly platform-db, mcp-server, api, frontend — no procrastinate worker service, and the api command (lines 43-45) runs only migrate + uvicorn. mcp_server/server.py:607 dispatches via `materialize_workspace.defer_async(...)`, which requires a worker to execute. On the compose stack every materialization stays 'todo' forever, reconcile returns None (tasks.py:748), and the job spins indefinitely. CLAUDE.md does advertise `docker compose up` as "All services".
votes.notes: Verdict CONFIRMED: every element of the chain reproduced independently. Two refinements rather than corrections: (a) the production-path defect is strictly trigger-dependent (hard worker death), so on its own it would be LATENT — but the docker-compose self-host path is broken on every single materialization today (no worker service exists), and per user memory the 2026-05-30 incident shows the prod trigger has already fired in practice, so BROKEN-NOW stands for the combined finding. (b) The finding understates one consequence: apps/agents/graph/base.py:318-332 means a zombie ACTIVE MaterializationRun also makes the agent refuse to start any new materialization ("Do NOT trigger another one"), not just freeze the progress UI. Minor nit: the cited file:line jobs_views.py:129 is the active-jobs poll's reconcile call, not the cancel endpoint; the actual active-count miscounting is materialization_views.py:46-54 — the substance of the claim is still correct. Suggested fixes for the parent: a periodic task calling app.job_manager.get_stalled_jobs() + retry_job (procrastinate 3.8 heartbeat-based), a MaterializationRun staleness janitor keyed off progress-update recency, `--shutdown-graceful-timeout` plus a Kamal stop grace in deploy-worker.yml, and a worker service in docker-compose.yml.
